title: Equivalent-neighbor $k$-core percolation in two dimensions
authors: Qiyuan Shi, Ming Li, Youjin Deng
abstract: We perform large-scale numerical simulations to investigate the critical behavior of $k$-core percolation in two dimensions with an extended interaction range $r$. By systematically varying both the core index $k$ and the interaction range $r$, we construct a comprehensive phase diagram in the $(k,r)$ plane. In contrast to $k$-core percolation in infinite dimensions, no hybrid transition is observed in two dimensions: the phase diagram contains only a continuous transition regime and a strictly first-order regime, separated by a tricritical or critical-end point $(k_s,r_s)$. For $k<k_s$ and $r<r_s$, the transition is continuous and belongs to the universality class of standard two-dimensional (2D) percolation. For $k>k_s$ and finite $r>r_s$, the transition is discontinuous, with no hybrid features or critical singularities. In this first-order regime, the pseudocritical point approaches the critical point as $1/\ln L$, where $L$ is the linear system size, distinct from the $L^{-d}$ scaling typical of conventional thermodynamic first-order transitions in $d$ dimensions. This logarithmic finite-size drift is consistent with a nucleation-driven mechanism, in which rare voids trigger the collapse of the finite-range $k$-core. These results demonstrate that geometric constraints can fundamentally alter the nature of $k$-core percolation found in finite dimensions.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the hybrid phase transition characteristic of infinite-dimensional k-core percolation does not exist in two dimensions. Instead, the system undergoes either a standard 2D percolation transition or a first-order transition driven by a nucleation mechanism. This reveals how geometric constraints in finite dimensions fundamentally alter the nature of phase transitions compared to mean-field predictions.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of the phase transition in 2D k-core percolation as the interaction range r varies, specifically determining if the hybrid transition seen in infinite dimensions persists in 2D.
main_result: The authors find that no hybrid transition exists in 2D; instead, the phase diagram contains only continuous (2D percolation universality) and first-order regimes, with the latter driven by a nucleation mechanism.
method: Large-scale numerical Monte Carlo simulations on triangular lattices using recursive pruning and a fast sampling algorithm, combined with finite-size scaling analysis.
model_or_system: An equivalent-neighbor k-core percolation model on a 2D triangular lattice where a site remains active if it has at least k neighbors within a Euclidean distance r.
key_observables: Order parameter (fraction of sites in the k-core), susceptibility, critical polynomial (wrapping probability difference), and fractal dimension.
important_parameters: Core index k, interaction range r, occupation probability p, and system size L.
main_assumptions: The study focuses on 2D triangular lattices and assumes the behavior is representative of the 2D regime, noting that the results for k-core percolation are sensitive to spatial dimension.
figures_summary: Figures show the phase diagram in the (k, r) plane, the bimodal distribution of the order parameter in the first-order regime, and the logarithmic finite-size scaling of the pseudocritical point.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the k-core problem and the mean-field limit, describes the numerical simulation methodology and sampling algorithms, presents the constructed phase diagram, analyzes the nature of the transitions (continuous vs. first-order), and discusses the renormalization-flow implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Bimodal distribution P(x) of the rescaled largest cluster size x ≡C1/V for 3-core percolation on CGs. All data are sampled at criticality, i.e., ER graphs with average degree qc ≈3.3509. With increasing V , the active branch narrows rapidly and its position approaches the theoretical jump value from above, reflecting the fast finite-size convergence of the surviving-core samples on the CG.

FIG. 2. Order parameter as a function of the average de- gree q for 3-core percolation on CGs. Panel (a) shows the results averaged over all samples, which leads to a smeared, seemingly continuous transition. Panel (b) shows the con- ditional average m+ obtained using samples with C1 > 0, revealing a clear discontinuous jump followed by a critical singularity. The slope near qc increases with system size, in excellent agreement with theoretical predictions m0 ≈0.2676 and qc ≈3.3509 (dashed lines). The inset of (b) plots m+(qc, V ) −m0 against V −1/4, testing the finite-size form expected from β/ν = 1/4.

FIG. 4. Phase diagram of k-core percolation on the extended triangular lattice in the (k, r) parameter space. A special line (ks, rs) separates a continuous transition regime (red circles), which belongs to the universality class of standard 2D perco- lation, from a first-order regime (blue squares). No evidence of a hybrid transition is found in two dimensions. The phase diagram is obtained from simulations with system size up to L = 4096. Points in the shaded region, while suggestive of a continuous transition at the current level of precision, cannot be unambiguously classified due to strong finite-size effects. For r →∞, the system reduces to the CG, for which the hy- brid transition...

FIG. 3. Finite-size behaviors of the conditional order pa- rameter m+ and susceptibility χ+ near criticality of 3-core percolation on CGs. The data are obtained by restricting the averages to samples with C1 > 0. Excellent data collapse is achieved using the mean-field exponents β = 1/2, γ = 1, and ν = 2. The lines indicate the scalings, ˜m(x) ∼xβ, ˜χ(x) ∼ x−γ, for x ≫1.

FIG. 5. Finite-size scaling of k-core percolation on triangular lattices (r = 1). Panels (a) and (b) show the critical poly- nomial PB as a function of the occupation probability p near the transition for k = 2 and k = 3, respectively. The curves for different system sizes L intersect at well-defined points, al- lowing for a precise determination of the thresholds pc. Panel (c) shows the size of the largest k-core cluster C1 at pc as a function of L, where the slope yields a fractal dimension df ≈1.8958, consistent with the exact value df = 91/48 for standard 2D percolation.
